I still don't get it. The only way I can see that making the loop "cycle
faster" like this would help is if there's some latency-sensitive
operations that are performed once per cycle. What are those? Cancellation
for example just schedules a task to run; if the task being cancelled can't
get onto the ready queue then it doesn't matter how fast the ready queue
cycles. sleep(0) right now explicitly takes the task *off* the ready queue.

On Nov 20, 2016 12:32 PM, "Alexander Zhukov" <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

@njsmith <https://github.com/njsmith> , I know, this seems like a paradox,
but this is true. The less tasks we schedule to run in a single turn, the
more often a loop will cycle. Say, we already have 1000 tasks ready to run.
If run them in one batch, the loop will be busy servicing this 1000 tasks,
thus it kinda "blocks" until the ready queue is exhausted. That means, the
loop is unresponsive to other tasks, like task cancellations and friends.
This is also related to the ability to voluntarily preempt tasks (see #106
<https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/106>), since the faster the loop
cycles, the faster it will continue to service the client after sleep(0)
call. Basically, if we know, say from some benchmark, that the throughput
of the loop is some N tasks per second, we shouldn't load the loop with
more than that.

This looks to be confusion between the faraday constant, which does indeed have a dimension of `[charge]/[substance]`, and the faraday unit of charge which has a dimension of `[charge]` and is equal to the charge of exactly one mole of electrons.  Thus the faraday constant is `1 faraday_unit/mole`.

See this page for some background which includes discussion of those obsolete pysical and chemical faradays which previously relied on different definitions of atomic wight before the amu and mole were standardized. 

https://sizes.com/units/faraday.htm

So I think the correct solution is to define `faraday_constant= faraday/mole`.
I think it would be a nice addition. Any thoughts against this?

```
from faker import Factory

class MyFactory(Factory):

    def something(self):
        return 'i work!'

MyFaker = MyFactory.create

f = MyFaker()

print(f.name())
print(f.something())
```

The reason for the ctypes version was for use on systems where compiling
the c-module is not possible but the openssl library exists. The primary
environment for which that is useful today is Pypy. If I recall correctly,
there's only a minimal performance difference between the native C module
and the ctypes version so if I were going to drop one, it'd probably be the
C module. It's worth discussing though. With respect to Cython, I thought
about that a couple years ago but ruled it out as it's not available
everywhere. I haven't looked at it again since though so if things have
changed, it might be worth reconsidering that stance.

@jamestwebber sorry about the delay in my response. This is what the `--ami-tags` option is for. In EC2, resource names are actually just tags (key=value pairs) with the magical key `Name`. So you should be able to do what you want with `aegea launch --ami-tags Name=czbiohub-something`. From all the AMIs tagged with a given tag, aegea will choose the latest and launch that. You shouldn't need to put a date in your AMI name, since the creation date is already present among the metadata properties of the AMI.

I implemented almost what I wrote before, but kept the file reading in the main TCL file. After having thought a bit about it, I think that would work just as well.

The number of properties set by FuseSoC is reduced to a minimum. FuseSoC will now
1. create the project
2. Set `part` if that is defined in the Vivado options
3. Enable vhdl2008 parsing if any VHDL-2008 files are detected (this option is always enabled from Vivado 2016.1 so it will have no effect for modern Vivado versions)
4. Set generics, parameters and defines
5. Read all source (and TCL files)
6. Set include directories
7. Set toplevel is that is defined in the core file
8. Upgrade all IPs

So, in theory, everything that is set before 5 can be overridden by a custom TCL file, so the only thing that would be out of your control are step 6 and 8. Let me know if there are any valid reasons to not always do them.

Running is deferred to another TCL file. More on that later.



> I think the approach is ok and will work. When you take the order in the core file into account, I‘m wondering why you see a problem in executing tcl files before adding rtl. On the other side I currently have no strict requirement in running command before rtl. Most of my additions can also be executed after rtl adding, maybe with the exeception of setting the default_lib property. But I learned in the meantime that this is not really needed.

Yeah. The default lib thing was the first potential problem I could see yesterday. I tried it though, and it works fine to set it after files are read.


> As you can see in my example code, I only set properties on [current_project]. Maybe it is possible to add a more generic property setup mechanism to the vivado section. They follow all the same schema and a lot of things in Vivado are set up with properties.

My philosophy here is that I want to avoid adding code for things that can be done in a more generic way. This keeps the FuseSoC code base small and easier to manage. This is a good example as you can just as well write a TCL file, even if it's slighly more code.

> Wrapping the tcl scripts with a makefile has its pros and cons. I think you use the approach already with the simulations. The advantage is of course the ability to specify different targets. The disadvantage is that it is not possible to run the makefile out of Vivado tcl console. Currently I start Vivado without opening a project, cd to the build dir, and source the script. When something is wrong, I close the project, rerun fusesoc (which as a side effect cleans the build dir) and then source the script again. This is quite fast because I save the time to load Vivado into memory.

All the logic is still kept in the TCL files. There are three targets in the makefile, of which two are used by FuseSoC and the third is currently intended to be run manually by someone who stops after the `--setup` stage.

First target creates the xpr file by sourcing the TCL script mentioned in the beginning. Second target will open this project and run the implementation flow including writing the bitstream

Third target, called `build-gui`, will launch Vivado in GUI mode and create the xpr file first if needed. To get the same behaviour as previously, just source `your_core.tcl` and then `your_core_run.tcl`




> On the other side, when using makefiles, it may be an idea to go further with this concept and for example offer more targets:
> 
>     Build in batch

Done
>     Create project interactively without running implementation

Done
>     Create project for interactive debug/simulation (in fact this would be more comfortable than having separate build and simulation workflows as today)

I have considered this too, but as Vivado really is the only tool (plus perhaps ISE) that offers a combined implementation/simulation shell, it will get quite tricky to get that right.

>     Program bitfile

Ah, right. The programming TCL file only gets written during the programming stage. We should just write it out in the configure phase instead. That way you can just source it from Vivado

>     ... all sorts of other ideas
> 
> So when reasoning about the potentials the makefile approach will pay out eventually.

Please try the latest patches. Interested to hear if that solves your case. Thanks for the feedback and discussion. Other people's use cases are very important to move FuseSoC forward
Oh and also, thanks for working on this so quickly!

Right, lists and generators are iterables. But this:
```
x = [1, 2, 3]
```
is different from 
```
y = [
   [1],
   [2],
   [3],
]
```

`x` is a list of integers, `y` is a list of lists. Sure, the nested lists in `y` are themselves iterables of integers, but `y` itself is a list of lists. Or an iterable of iterables, you could say. More specifically, an iterable of iterables of integers.

As you point out, in your example `featureFamily` is a list. That's what's being yielded each time through the loop in the generator function. So in the original example, `generatorModifyFeatures`  is an generator of lists -- an iterable of iterables like `y` above. The fix was to convert it into an iterable of `Feature`s, sort of like converting `y` to `x`.

Hi Robert,

Plyplus has trouble with this grammar because of token collision. The two tokens 'a' and '[a]' match the exact same input (namely, 'a'). That means only the token with the highest priority will be recognized by the lexer  (in this case: '[a']).

The error you get while parsing is because Plyplus expected the token 'a', but got the token '[a]'.

If you change it to '[b]' in the grammar, and input 'ab', you will see that it works as intended.

In general, it's recommended to avoid overlapping tokens, since it can produce confusing errors. But sometimes it's useful, like when matching both '+' and '++'. In general, the longest token always takes priority.

If you have overlapping tokens in your grammar, and can't find an obvious workaround, let me know. I can suggest several methods to solve it.

> because the OID is perfectly in the scope of the requested OID.

You mean we should not report that EOM oid-value at the end?

Trouble is that if you walk 1+ OIDs at the same time, the EOM condition for different OIDs can happen at different steps. In that case we are kind of forced to fill that EOM sentinel for the value of EOM OIDs for as long as we have at least one OID not reaching its EOM...

I think the reason this wasn't implemented originally was based primarily on the typical use case.

For instance, a normal JWT use case is for an identity provider (the issuer) to issue a token to a user that is valid for service A, service B, and service C. When service A receives a request containing the token, it doesn't care about whether or not service B or service C are in the list, only that it (service A) is in the list of audiences on the token.

For that reason, I think most use cases only require validating that a single audience is in the list.

One small thing first. You don't need to include the `headers` value in your call. Those headers will be automatically populated by PyJWT when it is creating the token. The `headers` parameter is for adding additional header parameters besides just those required by the spec.

Regarding your actual issue... some time ago we switched to using `cryptography` as the preferred cryptographic library for PyJWT. We switched for a number of reasons (most notably: the fact that cryptography is a much better implementation) I strongly recommend that, if possible, you run `pip install cryptography` and install the cryptography package. That will resolve the issue.

If you are unable to install `cryptography` we do have legacy support for PyCrypto but this is not recommended unless you are on a platform (like Google App Engine) that doesn't allow you to use `cryptography`.

You can setup the legacy support for algorithms by doing the following:
```
>>> import jwt
>>> from jwt.contrib.algorithms.pycrypto import RSAAlgorithm
>>> jwt.register_algorithm('RS256', RSAAlgorithm(RSAAlgorithm.SHA256))
>>> jwt.encode(claim, private_key, algorithm='RS256')
```

I managed to hack something together on the train today. So the idea would be to parse some form of input (markdown, html etc) into a kind of AST, then feed that into a renderer to actually produce output. With this we get support for Markdown as well as HTML, and support for multiple renderers (like maybe one that modifies a .docx file directly, rather than use the COM interface?)

[I broke the tags down into operations](https://github.com/orf/HtmlToWord/blob/redesign/HtmlToWord/operations.py) that form a tree. We keep the actual rendering code away from these classes and use them just to store info about the operation (like an images source or a hyperlink location). Then we have a [simple HTML parser](https://github.com/orf/HtmlToWord/blob/redesign/HtmlToWord/parser/html.py#L30) that makes a list of operations to execute, which can be fed to our [COM renderer](https://github.com/orf/HtmlToWord/blob/redesign/HtmlToWord/render/com.py) which will execute each operation. 

It uses a little [decorator magic](https://github.com/orf/HtmlToWord/blob/redesign/HtmlToWord/render/__init__.py#L8) to get rid of boilerplate. I also don't like the idea of separate StartRender() and EndRender() methods, passing state between them is hard. So we can make each of our rendering methods a generator that yields once indicating that all children should be rendered before execution continues. So we can just store the start of a range as a local variable and stuff.

Any feedback on the design is welcomed :)

One thing that might be confusing is that ``accept_untrusted_content = True`` has no effect if the sender specifically included a ``hash``. I think I did this because that's how the Node lib chooses to send an unhashed request (it doesn't specify a hash at all). When the hash is present it seems like it's wrong to ignore the hash. By saying "allow untrusted content" it's really saying allow a request that does not have a content hash but still continue to disallow mis-matching hashes if one is present.

While I did find one or two bugs that were not being tripped I think I also found the main problem.  The interleaved data reader is miscalculating the size of a data chunk and reading too much data.  When the code attempts to reshape the oversized array returned from np.fromfile it throws an exception because the array is not an even multiple of the size of one multi-channel sample.

I believe the following update fixes the bug.  I have not yet verified that the new code reads _all_ the data in the TDMS file.  

I also found a couple other problems.  The bigger one is that the spec says there can be more than one 'format changing scaler' but the code in the patch assumes there is always exactly one.  I have no idea what a 'format changing scaler' is, and the data related to them is never used, but the code should handle this so that it doesn't misread data that comes after the scaler specifications.

I will probably play around with the updated code for a while and post either an updated patch or a pull request.
```
         """Read interleaved data where all channels have a numpy type"""
 
         log.debug("Reading interleaved data all at once")
         # Read all data into 1-byte unsigned ints first
         all_channel_bytes = sum((o.data_type.length for o in data_objects))
-        number_bytes = all_channel_bytes * data_objects[0].number_values
+        number_bytes = all_channel_bytes * data_objects[0].tdms_object.number_values
         combined_data = fromfile(f, dtype=np.uint8, count=number_bytes)
```

The above change is in code that is likely used by the legacy TDMS reader so this change could break reading legacy-formatted TDMS files.

Hi. `colorama.init` wraps stdout and stderr. Your StringIO object doesn't get wrapped by colorama. What happens here is that you print ANSI codes into the file, and only then print the content along with the ANSI codes to stdout - at that point, colorama does its magic (this would work on Windows too) but the "autoreset" will happen only after both lines are printed. There is a workaround if you want to explicitly wrap your stream:

    buf = colorama.initialise.wrap_stream(buf, convert=None, strip=False, autoreset=True, wrap=True)

You might also want to test acme-tiny against [Pebble](https://github.com/letsencrypt/Pebble), a standalone ACME v2 testing server. Actually, your branch will most likely fail when talking to it since it actively ignores some valid nonces, while your `_send_signed_request` does not retry on the `urn:ietf:params:acme:error:badNonce` error; see [the explanation for Pebble here](https://github.com/letsencrypt/Pebble#invalid-anti-replay-nonce-errors).

Also, when you want to try Pebble: the directory endpoint is `https://localhost:14000/dir` in the default config, i.e. it doesn't end with `/directory`. So `/directory` (resp. `/dir` in this case) should probably be part of the `DEFAULT_CA` string.

@NiekKeijzer Sorry for my earlier response. I went back to look at the code and the tests (in particular look at `tests/test_initialize.py` ([Here](https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt/blob/dev/tests/test_initialize.py#L81) and [here](https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt/blob/dev/tests/test_initialize.py#L90)).

This is clearly a deficiency in the exception message, and the documentation.

Thinking about this further, your above initialization method was exactly as I had wanted it to be:

```python
Initialize(bp, authenticate=lambda: True)
```

That is simple and easy. The problem is that the `Blueprint` object is not itself aware of the `Sanic` object it is attached to. Therefore, we **DO** need the `app`, and your original initialization **WAS** correct.

======

So, forgive me and let me back up, because I do remember what is going on here now.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/166269/39562520-c4a4a4a0-4eb4-11e8-8543-bcbfc183de80.png)

In your first example, you attach the blueprint to the app **after** initialization. This causes this to happen. If you swap the ordering, it will appear as expected:

```python
from sanic import Sanic, Blueprint
from sanic_jwt import Initialize

app = Sanic()
bp = Blueprint('bp', url_prefix='/api')
app.blueprint(bp)

Initialize(
    instance=bp,
    app=app,
    authenticate=lambda: True
)

print('app', app.router.routes_all.keys())
print('bp', [x.uri for x in bp.routes])

# Result:
app dict_keys([])
bp ['/api/auth/', '/api/auth/me', '/api/auth/verify']
```

Is this a bug? Maybe. Unless there is a reason to not have `Initialize` after you register your `Blueprint`. If I remember correctly, this was due to a limitation I came across with Sanic. If there ends up being a good reason that we need to overcome this, I am open to making a change. Otherwise, I see this as staying as is for now (except, perhaps with better docs).

[See message here](https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt/pull/58#issuecomment-367139800). I got around this issue by passing the instance to `@protected`. Not a final resolution, but a working solution for v1.

Hi Dan, this looks pretty good. A couple remarks.

* Generally we like to start with an enhancement proposal, usually in the form of an analysis page. There's the skeleton of one for Group Shape here:
  https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/docs/dev/analysis/features/shp-group-shape.rst

  The key question I expect will be to determine the API. So in this case it looks like you're proposing `GroupShape`, `shape.is_group`, and `GroupShape.shapes`. It probably makes sense to lay this out in the analysis page so we can discuss. The API is the critical piece, because we can't change it once it's released. So investing the time up-front to get that right is a must-do.

* We'll need an acceptance test or two to drive the merge. However it gets written, it gets merged as atomic unit-test/code commits that address the next error on an acceptance test. I'm thinking something like what you find in `features/shp-autoshape-props.feature`, put into a new feature file `shp-groupshape-props.feature`, and testing that `GroupShape.shapes` is a `Shapes` object for a start.

* I expect there will be a couple more unit tests involved, but the acceptance test really determines that.

* I'm just finishing up some refactoring that will impact this. No action on that just now, just something to note. We should be able to slot this in when we get to it with just a little re-wiring around the edges :)

Just an idea on the .is_shape bit, I'm thinking that identifying a group shape is best done with shape.type, which would return `MSO_SHAPE_TYPE.GROUP` for a group shape. That way type identification is uniform across shape types. I might be persuaded that it makes sense to have both though :)

Also, I notice that the MS API uses .GroupItems instead of .Shapes on their group shape. I think we want to understand why they thought it wise to use a distinct member (rather than reuse .Shapes) for that job. Generally these little non-intuitive characteristics point up a design point that's not initially obvious so we should satisfy ourselves it's not distinct in some way before we bake it into the API.

This is what I came up with. It replaces the placeholder with the image, setting the max size to the bounds of the placeholder and preserving aspect ratio. It also centers the image in the placeholder.

```python
def replace_with_image(img, shape, slide):
    pic = slide.shapes.add_picture(img, shape.left, shape.top)

    #calculate max width/height for target size
    ratio = min(shape.width / float(pic.width), shape.height / float(pic.height))

    pic.height = int(pic.height * ratio)
    pic.width = int(pic.width * ratio)

    pic.left = shape.left + ((shape.width - pic.width) / 2)
    pic.top = shape.top + ((shape.height - pic.height) / 2)

    placeholder = shape.element
    placeholder.getparent().remove(placeholder)
    return
```

I thought about using `GetAll()` and putting everything in the new `format`, but like you said, it would lead to really complex string because of 80~ new placeholders, much longer format, and deprecating of placeholders + colors.

This module does not have `on/off` format so users can't add `on/off` icons if they want. At most, they only can add name or custom icon... and relies on `good/bad` colors.

Users can copy old `sshd` or `sshd.service` (colorized `sshd` or `sshd.service`) with new format.
With new format, users can make `sshd on` or `sshd off` (non-colorized `sshd`, colorized `on/off`).

```python
systemd {
    format = '{unit} [\?if=ActiveState=active&color=good on|\?color=bad off]'
}
```

This looks identical to your `hide_if_default = on` config. Not hardcoded.
```python
systemd {
    format = '[\?if=ActiveState=active '
    format += '[\?if=UnitFileState=disabled&color=good {unit}]]'
    format += '|[\?if=ActiveState=inactive '
    format += '[\?if=UnitFileState=enabled&color=bad {unit}]]'
}
```
or if you prefer without `.service`, swap out `{unit}` with `sshd` instead of using `hide_extension = True`. Another reason why `hide_extension` is meh is because the next new config could be `uppercase = True` versus using `SSHD` or even `SSH` in the format.

Things suggested here will be overkill for all users :face_with_head_bandage: but it would prevent more fairly-simple configs from being added and would offer more customization flexibility than before... even although the new `format` can be ugly sometimes. This also avoids fake placeholders (ie `{hide}` in this PR).

> Whatever solution we choose, I am a big fan of saving floating point data outside the [-1, 1) range, so this should definitely be preserved. This, might rule out using the sndfile flag.

The [documentation](http://www.mega-nerd.com/libsndfile/command.html#SFC_SET_CLIPPING) states:

"Turn on/off automatic clipping when doing floating point to integer conversion."

So this shouldn't be a problem.
I also tried it, and it indeed isn't a problem.

What I find a bit annoying is that it also silently changes the scaling behavior.
With the default setting (`SFC_SET_CLIPPING == SF_FALSE`), the values +-1.0 are mapped to +-32767 (for 16-bit PCM, as an example), so a signal within +-1.0 is never clipped, but very slightly scaled down.
When setting `SFC_SET_CLIPPING == SF_TRUE`, in addition to enabling clipping (as the name suggests), it also changes the scaling behavior (which is totally un-obvious).
Suddenly, the value +1.0 is clipped to `1.0-2**-15`, which is mapped to 32767 and the value -1.0 is directly mapped to -32768.
In other words, the signal is not scaled, but the value 1.0 cannot be represented anymore and is clipped.
Incidentally, this would be the behavior that @Whistler7 suggested in #20.

I would like to control the clipping behavior and the scaling behavior separately, but there seems to be no separate option for that in libsndfile.
Or am I missing something?

Anyway, there is no "right" solution for scaling, so we might as well choose the second one as default, even if I personally prefer the first one.

> As for integer normalization, I think that raising an error is the cleanest solution. In face of ambiguity, resist the temptation to guess.

Sure, that sounds reasonable.
But what price are we willing to pay for it?
Shall we really penalize the common correct usage in order to raise an error if the user makes a mistake?
I don't want to wait longer for my hypothetical 1 GB file to be written, just to check if the values are within +-1.0, which I most probably already know beforehand.

Hi, basically you need to sign something and check in side server that your sign is correct, so you need to extract the certificate that represent your sign, so in the server side you can check that the certificate is Valid in your trusted CA and then you can verify the sign.  

How to extract your certificate form device and convert to pem format
https://github.com/danni/python-pkcs11/compare/master...luisza:extract_certificates

You can send the signature in base64 and the pem certificate, then use the certificate to verify in server side,  For example you have a document sha256 hashsum that you need to sign, so you send it to your client, he sign the hashsum and send you the certificate and the signed message, you get that and check the certificate if it's ok, then try to verify the signed  sha256 hashsum  using the storage plain hashsum and the certificate.
 
The server side could be something like this:
```
from Crypto.Hash import SHA512
from Crypto.Signature import PKCS1_v1_5
from Crypto.PublicKey import RSA
def validate_sign(public_certificate, key, cipher_text):
    # check the certificate with your trusted CA
    if not check_certificate(public_certificate):
           raise Exception("Invalid certificate")
    cipher_text = b64decode(cipher_text)
    if hasattr(key, 'encode'):
        key = key.encode()
    digest = SHA512.new()
    digest.update(key)
    pub_key = RSA.importKey(public_certificate)
    verifier = PKCS1_v1_5.new(pub_key)
    result = verifier.verify(digest, cipher_text)
    return result
```

Here's some themes and variations on potential representations:
```python
# 1 - nothing
"ContractExpression('ij,jk,kl,lm,mn->ni', constants=(1, 2, 4))"

# 2 - individually wrapped
"ContractExpression('ij,[jk,kl],lm,[mn]->ni', constants=(1, 2, 4))"

# 3 - Moved to end
"ContractExpression('ij,lm[jk,kl,mn]->ni')"

# 4 - explicitly mentioned in contraction string #1
"ContractExpression('ij,lm->ni [Constant: jk,kl,mn]')"

# 5 - explicitly mentioned in contraction string #2
"ContractExpression('ij,lm,[Const:jk,kl,mn]->ni')"

# 6 - explicitly mentioned in contraction string #3
"ContractExpression('ij,lm,Const[jk,kl,mn]->ni')"
```
I'd favour 2 or maybe 5. Its certainly nice to keep the them in order, apart from making it less clear how many arguments the expression should now be called with (realistically if the user has created the expression they probably know though!).

Well, notice how `@download3/2` is a child of `@download/3/1`? The messages for the former are probably `[3, 2, 1]` and `[3, 2, 2]`.

The problem with the current numbering system is that it's a bit tricksy. `[1]` is the *start* message of the top-level action. `[2]` is a child message of top-level action; `[2, 1]` is the start message of a child action of top-level action.

That prose description is therefore slightly inaccurate; message 1 isn't really child, it's start message.

Neither NTFS, exFAT, or FAT32 (the filesystems Windows supports) store MIME media types. Windows relies entirely on filename extensions for opener associations. The only file systems that store MIME media types are: f2fs (Linux), ext3/ext4 (Linux), HFS+ (OS X), ZFS (Linux & OS X), and a many other less-popular filesystems (none of which are supported by Windows). Windows discards MIME media types prior to saving.

If you want to view your JSON output directly in your browser on Windows, you should rename the filename extension to `.json`.

Unfortunately not. Excel removes the rich text formatting from string in the linked to cell.

Happy with version `1.4.0`? I guess I'd call this a "backward compatible change of functionality" which semver.org would regard as a minor version bump.

> But still I'd be better for breaking the backward compatibility and for forcing users to migrate the sqlite explicitly by separate python script

I have software that uses sqlitedict as a backend -- the users you would force in such a case were not even aware of sqlitedict until it breaks! As long as the script is distributed and accessible as a module, I could always write "auto-upgrade" process downstream along with being able to use unicode as original intended.

My largest fear is the common mistake of people adding "sqlitedict" only as a requirement in setup.py or requirements.txt -- their package suddenly breaks against their existing data or code that assumes bytes or py2.7, not unicode.  Even worse, "users" may pin their direct dependencies, but the transient dependencies they require may not freeze, either.

The more involved it gets, the more in favor of the original proposal https://github.com/piskvorky/sqlitedict/issues/19#issuecomment-67394915 of simply distributing a new python package all together (eg. sqlitedict2) :)

You have two options for setting up tests.
1. Create a mock object and add the adapters you want to test with to it.
You may be able to do this by doing something like:
```python
context = Adaptation()
context.add_adapter(my_io_adapter)
context.add_adapter(my_storage_adapter)
...
```

2. Test at a higher level.
Create a test ChatBot() instance and specify only your adapter as a parameter.

Hello Amir,

num_operations is the number of BPE merge operations that are learned. 
The final vocabulary is the character vocabulary + num_operations.

Are you by any chance applying BPE to a text that is not word-segmented, 
such as Chinese? The code first extracts the word vocabulary, then 
learns the operations on this vocabulary, without allowing cross-word 
merging of symbols. A text without word segmentation breaks this assumption.

best wishes,

Some arguments for 1024 = KiB / 1000 = KB:

* `docker stats` uses MiB/GiB units
* the json stats are in bytes
* pnp4nagios has helper function to convert to 1000 or 1024 basis: https://docs.pnp4nagios.org/pnp-0.6/tpl_helper_pnp, so the conversions can be defered to the frontend (keep perfdata in B)
* there was some discussion about the disk checks yielding wrong units here: https://sourceforge.net/p/pnp4nagios/mailman/message/15564564/
* misleading units lead to bigger and bigger differences between 1000 and 1024-base measures: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_prefix#Deviation_between_powers_of_1024_and_powers_of_1000
* extreme example of confusion between units: the "1.44 MB" floppy disks holds 1.44 * 1000 * 1024 bytes

you mean `wormhole send FILE` and `wormhole send-text TEXT`, right? We don't currently have any polymorphic "keyword" arguments (`--something=SOMETHINGELSE`). There is no argument that might sometimes be a filepath and at other times might be a text string to be sent. And the only polymorphism we've got in the "positional" arguments is whether it's present or not. The current CLI is just:

* `wormhole send FILE`
* `wormhole send DIRECTORY`
* `wormhole send --text TEXT`

(note that in the text case, there are no positional arguments)

That said, I'm open to considering separate subcommands for sending filesystem things and sending text strings, as long as it doesn't make the commands too verbose.

**Assuming you want to dynamically generate code**

You could pass a reference to a function:

```
# python
{
    onClickMethod: ['./some-file', 'someMethod']
}
```

```
// JS
let onClickMethod = require(props.onClickMethod[0])[props.onClickMethod[1]);
```

This will trip up bundlers (webpack, etc) though, as the `require` call can't be statically analysed. It also seems like a generally bad idea from an architectural perspective 😄 

I suppose you could pass a function as a string and eval it.

I'd recommend just passing a flag down and having your JS act on that.

------------------------------------------------

**Assuming you want to have python code trigger based on the front-end's interactions**

Not from python code as the props need to be JSON serialisable and the front-end wont have any understanding of how to signal the back-end.

You'll need to wire up a server that can either handle requests or a socket connection so that your front-end code can talk back to the python process. You'll also need to handle how the front-end signals the backend.

> Could you please tell me what are the minimum requirements on how the image to be captured ?

It's a frontal face detector, so it won't detect side views of faces in most cases.

> I used the default model as specified in docs as dlib_face_recognition_resnet_model_v1.dat. Does changing this to another model help me in identifying the side views better ?

That's the face encoder model for *face recognition* that you are talking about. You are really asking about the *face detection* model which is a separate model. There are two built-in face detection models. You can switch to the second model by passing in `model="cnn"` to the `face_locations()` function. It will probably do a little better on side views of faces. You can see if that works for your data, but no promises that it will.

> As a human , i can say that a person is same with his side view after seeing his face color, face hair, face earings or piercings etc., Does models used in this library are already trained with all these features ? If so, why my 90degrees images are not identified as faces ? If not, could you please give me an example of how to train model with these features ?

It's not trained on side views since that's not really the use case of this library. If you want to build a face detection model that recognizes face side views really well, you'll need to train your own model. You can bypass this module and just use `dlib` directly to do that. This article will get you started: http://blog.dlib.net/2016/10/easily-create-high-quality-object.html

However if you use a CNN model in dlib, you'll have to write or tweak some C++ code to get it done. But there are some good examples in dlib that would work with very minimal tweaking.

sure:

```r
library(dplyr)
library(forcats)
library(survival)

N <- 20
df <- data_frame(
  time=rexp(N),
  event=as.integer(rbinom(N, 1, .5)),
  treatment_=c(rep("control", N/2), rep("active", N/2)))

cox <- coxph(Surv(time, event) ~ treatment_, data=df)
```

In the model summary below, "treatment_control" is HR of control:active , where active is the "reference" since it is alphabetically before "control"

```r
> cox
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(time, event) ~ treatment_, data = df)

                   coef exp(coef) se(coef)     z    p
treatment_control -0.373     0.689    0.917 -0.41 0.68
```

And **I'd like to see the reverse HR: active to control**, where control is the reference ((common in pharma/biotech)
```r
df$treatment_  <- df$treatment_ %>% as.factor() %>% forcats::fct_rev()
cox <- coxph(Surv(time, event) ~ treatment_, data=df)
```
```r
> cox
Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(time, event) ~ treatment_, data = df)

                 coef exp(coef) se(coef)    z    p
treatment_active 0.373     1.452    0.917 0.41 0.68
```

Now, this shows me the desired hazard ratio, which I obtained by reversing the "treatment_" factor variable via `fct_rev()`

(It is actually easy to get this hazard ratio: `exp(coef)` is just `1/exp(coef)` from the first cox model )

The issue was that in the requirements.txt file there is a dependency on lxml and that dependency is setup to be installed without-threading so it looked like this

lxml==3.5.0 --install-option='--without-threading'

Any time --install-option (or --build-options or --global-options) are passed, that disables all use of wheels.

Our solution was to move the installation of `lxml` into a separate build step.

If you are doing a POST to login and get a JWT, having CSRF protection there probably does not matter very much. If you want to include it, by all means, but assuming your login endpoint isn't allowing you to do anything else besides login with a username and password, being able to call that endpoint in a cross request forgery way, sending in the cookies that are already set in your application, shouldn't cause any security concerns. If it were me, I would see if I could disable CSRF protection for that one endpoint. Or alternately break the app and api layers out into different blueprints, and see if you could apply the various CSRF protection models only to the blueprints that required them (not sure if that is possible with those extensions or not, but might be worth looking into).

@sripathikrishnan i think the parser can provide 3 levels of callbacks, so that each implementation of the callback can decide which one it listens to.
1) high level per item callbacks such as HSET and LPUSH
2) low level bytes buffer payload of each key, with which you can send RESTORE command.
3) medium level per ziplist / listpack callbacks, these are useful in order to easily compute the memory footprint in many cases. (estimating memory footprint of a ziplist from individual items, is both complicated and possibly inaccurate)

so for instance, the default implementation of listpack or ziplist can be one that unpacks the listpack or ziplist and calls individual HSET / LPUSH / XADD callbacks.
but if the user overrides that callback, he can use the raw ziplist or listpack as is, and also reduce the need / effort of unpacking them.

please note that what we recently added for modules callbacks (and even skip_object) can somewhat be considered the foundation of collecting the RESTORE payload (parsing just enough in order to buffer the bytes and know where to stop).

Hello @kohillyang 
You are mixing Numpy.NDArray and MXNet.NDArray hence, seeing a different behavior.
### Numpy only
```
import numpy as np
>>> y = np.zeros(shape=(17,5))
>>> print(y.shape)
(17, 5)
>>> print(np.zeros_like(y).shape)
(17, 5)
```

### MXNet NDArray only

```
>>> import mxnet.ndarray as nd
>>> x = nd.zeros(shape=(17,5))
>>> print(x.shape)
(17, 5)
>>> print(nd.zeros_like(x).shape)
(17, 5)
```

You passed np.zeros_like(MXNet NDarray) which treat it as an array object. (x in your example is mxnet ndarray that cannot be directly understood by Numpy.

If you want to use MXNet NDArray inside numpy, here is the modified code for your example:

```
import numpy as np
import mxnet.ndarray as nd
import mxnet as mx
print(mx.__version__)
x = nd.zeros(shape=(17,5))
print(x.shape)
print(np.zeros_like(x.asnumpy()).shape) # Converting X to numpy
print(type(x))
print(nd.zeros_like(x).shape)
```

Will answer your questions in a second, but first I want to try to demonstrate the problem, as I think it is potentially simple.

This session indicates that when I touch an existing file in a directory on OSX, the "access" time changes, but the modify/change times do not. So it's possible that watchman is triggering an event for access time changes on directories?

```
[stuhood@tw ~/Desktop/stats]$ stat -x 3rdparty
  File: "3rdparty"
  Size: 68           FileType: Directory
  Mode: (0770/drwxrwx---)         Uid: (  501/ stuhood)  Gid: (   20/   staff)
Device: 1,4   Inode: 248704863    Links: 2
Access: Tue Jul 19 12:04:37 2016
Modify: Tue Jul 19 12:04:37 2016
Change: Tue Jul 19 12:04:37 2016
[stuhood@tw ~/Desktop/stats]$ cd 3rdparty/
[stuhood@tw ~/Desktop/stats/3rdparty]$ touch BUILD
[stuhood@tw ~/Desktop/stats/3rdparty]$ stat -x BUILD
  File: "BUILD"
  Size: 0            FileType: Regular File
  Mode: (0660/-rw-rw----)         Uid: (  501/ stuhood)  Gid: (   20/   staff)
Device: 1,4   Inode: 248704897    Links: 1
Access: Tue Jul 19 12:04:52 2016
Modify: Tue Jul 19 12:04:52 2016
Change: Tue Jul 19 12:04:52 2016
[stuhood@tw ~/Desktop/stats/3rdparty]$ stat -x .
  File: "."
  Size: 102          FileType: Directory
  Mode: (0770/drwxrwx---)         Uid: (  501/ stuhood)  Gid: (   20/   staff)
Device: 1,4   Inode: 248704863    Links: 3
Access: Tue Jul 19 12:04:37 2016
Modify: Tue Jul 19 12:04:52 2016
Change: Tue Jul 19 12:04:52 2016
[stuhood@tw ~/Desktop/stats/3rdparty]$ touch BUILD
[stuhood@tw ~/Desktop/stats/3rdparty]$ stat -x .
  File: "."
  Size: 102          FileType: Directory
  Mode: (0770/drwxrwx---)         Uid: (  501/ stuhood)  Gid: (   20/   staff)
Device: 1,4   Inode: 248704863    Links: 3
Access: Tue Jul 19 12:05:19 2016
Modify: Tue Jul 19 12:04:52 2016
Change: Tue Jul 19 12:04:52 2016
```

----

> At a higher level, is the goal "compute a directory listing using a set of globs as the tree changes"?

Correct, but that is a few levels of abstraction up. The fundamental operations are described below.

> Do you use the file change notification stream for more than just this?

We additionally invalidate file fingerprints/content that we hold in memory.

> Is it fair to say that you'd like to receive all file change notifications, but only directory notifications for deleted dirs?

No: we would want to receive an event for either creation, deletion, or child-changes of directories. We don't need to be able to distinguish between these events.

> Are you filtering out the dirs today because you need to perform a relatively expensive glob recomputation?

The original goal of filtering out the directories was to avoid the bug described by this (#305) issue. It seems very likely that there is a simple solution to this issue (#305) if we're able to agree on its definition.

----

In summary: pants implements all other operations in terms of the following operations:

1. Get a DirectoryListing for a Dir - children and their types (but _not_ their "clocks")
2. Get FileContent for a File - content of a file (but _not_ its "clock")
3. Get a ReadLink for a Link - read the path represented by a link, non-recursively

We use watchman to watch for events that affect the results of those individual operations (and then we transitively invalidate things that depended on the operations).

The issue described here is that touching an existing regular file triggers an event for its parent directory as well. It's not clear why this fires, as changing the mod/access time of a child file should not affect the mod/access time of the parent directory.

Hi @ssundara-nw,

as stated in the first link of the answer in the stackoverflow post, you can still retrieve the performance metrics of adobjects by directly addressing them using their id, but not by querying an upper level object's descendants:

> If you keep the deleted object id, you can continue to retrieve the stats or object details by individually querying the object ID. However you cannot retrieve the deleted objects as a connection object from a non deleted node/object. e.g. `<API_VERSION>/<AD_ID>/insights` works for a deleted object but `<API_VERSION>/act_<AD_ACCOUNT_ID>/insights?level=ad` will not return stats for the deleted object.

https://developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/best-practices/storing_adobjects#deleted

As a workaround I see three possible scenarios: You could either store the history of the structure and query all missing ads directly, store the history of the performance data directly and upsert this, or calculate the difference between the upper level object's performance and the aggregate of the individual objects (this will only work for certain measures obviously).

@leandromoreira I was thinking of modifying the `parse` function's signature from:

```python
def parse(content, strict=False)
``` 

To something along the lines of:

```python
def parse(content, strict=False, accept_extended_tags=False)
```

The default behavior of the function would remain the same, but with `accept_extended_tags` set to `True`,  a script such as 

```python
from m3u8 import parse

content = """#EXTM3U
#EXTINF:-1 tvg-id="Be1.be" parent-code="1234",Be1 Name
http://channel.stream.address/to_be_placed/on_separate_line/123.ts"""

# Prints content's first segment
print(parse(content)['segments'][0])
```

would print `{'title': 'Be1 Name', 'duration': -1.0, 'cue_out': False, 'uri': 'http://channel.stream.address/to_be_placed/on_separate_line/123.ts', 'discontinuity': False, 'extended_extinf_tags' : {'tvg-id': 'Be1.be', 'parent-code': '1234'}}` instead of rising a `ValueError` as it does now.

As far as I can tell, this would mainly imply a couple of minor modifications to `parse` and `_parse_extinf` and adding a couple tests.

When you don't specify a primary key in your models django will add an AutoField which translates to an integer column type. That means that you can't have more than 2147483647 in that table, otherwise bad things happen. I have found 2 solutions to this problem:
- use the new django 1.8 UUIDField as primaryKey
- use a big integer as a primary key. I'm adding support for it as part of https://github.com/mozilla/treeherder/pull/853.

OK, I think I found a good work-around for this issue.

Instead of using the named filter parameters like `InstanceIds` or `GroupIds`, always use `Filters`.

For example, to filter instances:

```python
>>> list(ec2.instances.filter(InstanceIds=[]))
<returns all instances>
>>> list(ec2.instances.filter(Filters=[{'Name': 'instance-id', 'Values': []}]))
[]
```

And to filter security groups:

```python
>>> list(ec2.security_groups.filter(GroupIds=[]))
<returns all security groups>
>>> list(ec2.security_groups.filter(Filters=[{'Name': 'group-id', 'Values': []}]))
[]
```

I still think it really sucks that `InstanceIds=[]` returns all instances. It's surprising and, since dynamically determined filters can always return an empty list, it's also dangerous. (i.e. You aren't normally going to explicitly specify `[]` as your filter. But `[]` can always be the result of, e.g. a list comprehension.)

However, at least we have a different pattern to use that avoids this issue.

This can obviously be avoided by just sending a smaller payload, but the issue is that botocore is unable to handle the sporadic cases in which AWS does not return json. Potential solutions would be:
 - catch the exception in `_parse_body_as_json` and return it in a `{ "message": ... }` structure
 - catch the exception in `_do_error_parse`and set `error['Error']['Message'] = response['body']`

Since the the exception_middlewares are used in the [reversed order](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/handlers/base.py#L38) they are put in settings.MIDDLEWARE, our middleware is the last one to handle the exception and if an other one handle the exception, it will never arrive to us.

I see 2 solutions:
1/ Patch this [function](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/handlers/base.py#L164-L173) to set the trace when the exception is raised
2/ Or use an other middleware that we would put at the end of settings.Middleware in order to be the first to get the exceptions (my favorite solution)
my solution is needed for distributed tracing in sub-processes (which I use extensively).  I can add another test case for this.  Thoughts, alternative solutions?

```
        profile_manager = self.content.hostProfileManager
        spec = pyVmomi.vim.profile.host.HostProfile.SerializedHostProfileSpec()
        spec.profileConfigString = open(self.vpf_path, 'rb').read().decode()
        spec.name = self.name
        try:
            self.remote_obj = profile_manager.CreateProfile(spec)
        except pyVmomi.vim.fault.DuplicateName as e:
            self.remote_obj = e.object
```
Basically, try to create a new profile with the same name and the exception will return a reference to that existing profile (if you have a deterministic naming scheme)

The instance UUID can be blank but in an environment with over 50k VMs Ive
personally not seen that happen. VMs that make up a vApp can and do share
the same bios UUID which makes it less useful to use. The MORef and the
InstanceUUID are the only properties that will be unique. If you see the
docs on instanceUUID they say: "VirtualCenter-specific 128-bit UUID of a
virtual machine, represented as a hexademical string. This identifier is
used by VirtualCenter to uniquely identify all virtual machine instances,
including those that may share the same SMBIOS UUID." So instanceUUID is
what I use, but if you are worried about it being blank since the docs do
say it could be blank the MORef is the only other thing that is guaranteed
to be unique. Also if you are working with vCloud director and having to do
stuff between vSphere API and vCloud API to a vm in a vApp you will need to
use the MORef because the VCD API will give you that info and not the UUID
info..

-mike

Yes, they are correct in general. Two minor technical details:

* Lock does not always download the most recent. Say if I specify Django (latest is 2.0.x), but one of my dependencies require 1.11.x, it will download the latest 1.11.11 (currently the latest in 1.11 series). Might want to clear this up.
* Sync does not really download anything on its own. The cache is managed by pip, sync only tells it to install things. You can actually set `PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR` to disable it, and sync won’t care. Overall I think it would suffice to simply say sync hands the requirements in Pipfile.lock for pip to install them.

Really those are technical details, but better to do them absolutely right than risk giving people wrong impressions.

From your previous post: 

> @techalchemy I just upgraded to pipenv 2018.6.25 and I still see an issue with matplotlib when pipenv is resolving the +mkl numpy version.

_resolving_ is the key word.  Pipenv can't _resolve_ something unless _pip_ knows where to look for it, which is why I told you to set `PIP_FIND_LINKS` as an environment variable in order to solve the problem.  If pip encounters something that depends on numpy before it installs numpy as specified in your pipfile, it will attempt to _resolve_ numpy, which means it will do it the normal way.  We install things asynchronously, so sometimes this won't be an issue, but sometimes it will.  If you want to ensure that it isn't an issue, you need to set the environment variable which tells pip 'oh and by the way, you can also check this folder to resolve dependencies'.

Beyond that I'd encourage you to check out stackoverflow or the pip/pipenv docs, this is the solution to the issue at hand and if you have other bugs i'd encourage you to submit them in a new issue.  Thanks for your patience and for reporting the bug with `+` parsing!

Do we want to build a proper python / pip-installable interface to patchelf? It would be straightforward to either provide a python library / API that internally invoked the binary or perhaps a little trickier but still straightforward to modify the code to provide an API that we wrap from cython.

While CPython as a whole has many contributors, we *don't* have many folks contributing to `distutils` any more - folks need build tools that let them target currently *deployed* versions of Python, which `setuptools` provides, but `distutils` really doesn't.

So the challenge for #127 is as follows:

- assume that in some future version of Python (3.9? 3.10? 4.0? We dunno yet) `distutils` is no longer in the standard library per se, but is instead bundled the way we bundle `pip` (i.e. by installing it from a wheel at Python installation time).
- eventually, for *new* Python versions (where `distutils` isn't in the standard library), we'd like the "real" distutils code to live in `setuptools`, and the code you get when you do `import distutils` to just be a backward compatibility shim that aliases the `setuptools` components into the right place
- however, for *old* Python versions (where `distutils` is still in the standard library), then `setuptools` will still need to do its monkeypatching magic to allow plain distutils projects to emit the installation database metadata and to be built as wheel archives

The scope of the eventual `distutils-compat` shim probably wouldn't need to be the full distutils API, but it wouldn't be the empty set either.

So @jaraco's suggestion sounds like a reasonable starting point to me: give `setuptools` its own pre-patched copy of `distutils` so it can work on a `distutils`-free copy of Python, *without* providing an importable `distutils` facade.

Building a suitable facade (with a `setuptools` dependency) would then be part of removing `distutils` from the standard library.

./.pypirc
```

Another alternative is to use the [twine package](https://pypi.org/project/twine), which allows for the username to be specified on the command-line.

Not so much a suggestion but really just a statement that this is going to *currently* make installing setuptools via sdist via pip more annoying since people will have to manually install setuptools' build dependencies (since pip has no concept of that yet, and ``setup_requires`` won't work in this case).

That being said, https://github.com/pypa/pip/pull/4144 should hopefully land in the near future and allow setuptools to add a ``pyproject.toml`` file to itself that looks like:

```toml
[build-system]
requires = [
    "wheel",
    "packaging>=16.8",
    "six>=1.10.0",
    "appdirs>=1.4.0",
]
```

and pip will basically do what ``rwt setup.py`` would do, except with just those dependencies.

I'm also -1 on changing the default, *especially* changing the default only on Python 3. People find it hard enough to reason about writing a 2/3 compatible `setup.py` without this.

I think having *a way* to use `find_packages` with namespace packages is important, but having `find_packages` do it automatically is not worth the mental overhead this adds.

> I see all these recommendations to use setuptools instead of distutils, but then this basic requirement is still not supported.

Use `pip install` instead to install your project. You can still use `setuptools` inside the `setup.py` script, but `pip` changes some default installation directories such that `data_files` becomes supported just like it was with `distutils`.

> Setuptools is injecting default values

That characterization isn't correct. These aren't default values, but overrides. Setuptools is _replacing_ the user-supplied values, disabling 'tag_svn_revision' and 'tag_date', and instead materializing those values in 'tag_build'.

That makes sense, now that I think about it. You don't want those values being recalculated when the sdist is installed, but you want them materialized at build time so they're fixed to the build.

If in a subshell from within fish, the PATH variable will be converted to the proper format for sh, but none of the custom path-like variables will. This causes errors because it sets the PATH to an invalid value when activating.

This patch makes activate.fish always store the old path using : deliminators and then converts it back to fish format later.

Issue reproduction

Simplest example:

PATH converts correctly

fish $ echo $PATH
  /bin /usr/local/bin
fish $ bash -c 'echo $PATH'
  /bin:/usr/local/bin


OLD_PATH does not

Hi @hamedhsn - here's some example code to get you started

```python
client = KafkaClient(hosts="xxxxxxx")
topic = client.topics['mytopic']
consumer = topic.get_simple_consumer(
    auto_offset_reset=OffsetType.LATEST,
    reset_offset_on_start=True)
LAST_N_MESSAGES = 50
offsets = [(p, op.next_offset - LAST_N_MESSAGES) for p, op in consumer._partitions.iteritems()]
consumer.reset_offsets(offsets)
consumer.consume()
```
This code sets the consumer's offset to `LATEST`, then subtracts some arbitrary amount from each partition's offset and gives those values to the consumer. It's untested, but it gets the point across. Check out the `reset_offsets` and `OffsetType.LATEST` attributes on `SimpleConsumer`.

As I understand, it suggest `hostname:port` for producers and consumers. The default value is hostname. BUT if the client does not know this hostname, this error is emitted. 

Similar problem decribed here: https://github.com/wurstmeister/kafka-docker/issues/100#issuecomment-297815839

The solution for me was to add `remotehost` to `/etc/hosts`.

Nevertheless, this should be detected and the exception message should be way more clear.

@astrofrog - I think I've voiced that opinion before: I'm not sure `astropy.regions` should / needs to provide `to_shapely` and `from_shapely` methods and would suggest to just get rid of those. I think the fraction of users that will actually do this is very small (how many astronomers do you know that have actually used Shapely?) -- and if someone needs it, they can just write the relatively simple code to do the conversion themselves.
Note that development of this package towards something that can be proposed as `astropy.regions` for the core package has stalled, and the shapely conversion is something that can simply be added later if deemed useful, and just removing it here would let us concentrate a bit more on the core of the functionality. And because shapely has such a large dependency, I don't think that we should use it to implement any functionality, i.e. use it from any other methods.

So @astrofrog - you think it's useful and we should keep `shapely`, right?

@cdeil  Yes, this fixes a literal edge case.  It's a guarantee modulo possible float-point errors.  If `radius=4.5` gets stored as`4.50000001` then there's going to be an extra 1 pixel added to each side (top, bottom, left, right) of the bounding box (stored as `4.4999999999` will work as expected).  However, I don't think there's anything we can do about that.

I worry a bit about make `_from_float` public because it behaves somewhat differently from the `__init__` function.  `_from_float` adds a 1 to the upper bounds to account for the exclusive upper index.  The `__init__` function doesn't do this (it assumes you have already done it).

Example:

```python
>>> from regions import BoundingBox
>>> BoundingBox(ixmin=1, ixmax=10, iymin=2, iymax=20)
BoundingBox(ixmin=1, ixmax=10, iymin=2, iymax=20)

>>> BoundingBox._from_float(xmin=1, xmax=10, ymin=2, ymax=20)
BoundingBox(ixmin=1, ixmax=11, iymin=2, iymax=21)
```

That could be very confusing even though the difference is explained in the documentation.  If you want to make it public, perhaps `_from_float` should be renamed?

I don't disagree that this quirk should be documented somewhere, but I'm not sure what the best place would be. The issue is not specific to subscriptions (although subscriptions would probably be the most common usecase) and also I suspect not specific to Python -- it has to do with how our libraries handle updates on an attribute in a list object that's itself nested under another object. Basically the parent object does not know that an attribute was updated on the nested list object, and so calling `save()` on the parent object does nothing.

Just throwing it out there, but would simply making the change and documenting it in release notes be less disruptive to users than adding an unnecessary kwarg - so they can keep doing it wrong if they so choose - which then will disruptively break their workflow later?

Seems like a lot of trouble including two separate deprecations, a brand-new temporary kwarg with a shelf life, and branching output paths could be avoided if we just... did the right thing.

Put differently

* Path direct change to Tuple:  Potentially disruptive to a very small subset of users.  Documented well and they handle it.
* Option 3, with default Tuple: Immediate disruptive change to this same set of users.  They are then provided the option of not changing, and some will take it - ignoring our warning that it has a sunset.  Two versions later, another disruptive change for these folks (plus anyone else who decided to START using the wrong kwarg in the interim!).
* Option 3, with default List instead of Tuple for next two versions: Everyone gets warnings all the time until they start using a kwarg which is going to go away, creating more problems.  

There should be one, correct way.  My vote is just change this to Tuple directly and document it.

Based on the comments here I propose that we set the `seed` keyword-argument default value to `0` everywhere in both C++ and Python. *Please follow up soon if you disagree with this conclusion.*

There was some idea of defining this in a variable somewhere but using that variable consistently is not any more DRY than using `0` consistently. Not to mention that the seed has no meaningful value across algorithm implementations. So to avoid a bunch of unnecessary imports (and thus code dependencies) I am excluding that from the proposal. I like the idea of using interfaces where they benefit but objects that have seeds don't actually share any functions. A lot of the time they take the seed as a constructor parameter but that isn't something you can enforce with a parent class.

I'm surprised you're calling this method directly. It's a helper for other methods. It has very strict requirements.

Did you see this requirement? "The caller must correctly count the number of zeros in the selection"
https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/d6081081957d96c4c0167aaf2224af2df13e657b/src/nupic/math/SparseMatrix.hpp#L981

If at this point `numRemainingAvailable` is 0, that means the zeros were miscounted. This method relies on correct counts. Because it knows how many zeros are remaining, it can randomly select which ones to use, and it can do it in one pass. I think this `NTA_ASSERT` is correct -- if you ever run into it, you're calling the method with invalid input.

Maybe I'm wrong? If you think your input meets the requirements, could you share it? It would be useful to see an example valid input that hits this assert.

On behalf of the _JOIN2 Steering Group_:

First of all, non of the institutions within JOIN2 have any policy that prevents them from using non-GPL software or to contribute to projects with free, but non-GPL licences. So there is no formal issue from JOIN2 against a change in license.

However, the JOIN2 community believes that the envisioned change in the Invenio licensing scheme does not help to achieve the goals brought forward.  Especially, JOIN2 does not see how a change away from GPL addresses

> maximize the impact of Invenio for the benefit of society.

JOIN2 does also not share the opinion that

> a license change will drive an increased adoption of Invenio

The JOIN2 community believes, that adoption of Invenio is not hindered by using GPL as a licence, while JOIN2 believes that one can expect some cooperation, even from companies, who use for free what was achieved by

> CERN and EU (aka taxpayers) has invested significant efforts

JOIN2 want to stress that we especially like the fact that GPL is a viral license and this ensures that everyone (even the taxpayer) can profit from improvements. (In this regard JOIN2 even sympathizes with @jacquerie comment.) JOIN2 sees no real issues with GPL even for extensions around Invenio or businesses build on top of Invenio. JOIN2 is of the opinion that one can build viable business models on GPL software.

> We believe people contribute to Invenio because they realize a greater value by contributing than if kept private.

JOIN2 makes the point, that if this is the case there is no need to change the license at all. One can stay with GPL and also to make the clear political statement that contributions back to the project are welcome.  

JOIN2 can envision, however, some sort of dual licensing as common in other projects, if ever a need for another license than GPL arises.

Because it matches the ISO 8601 timestamp forma, `unaware` is presumed to be *already* in UTC:  `unaware2` doesn't, and so is presumed to be in the local timezone for the server.  E.g., consider this PythonScript:

```python
foo = context.ZopeTime('2016-06-15T07:00:00')
bar = context.ZopeTime('2016/06/15 07:00:00')
print 'foo:', foo
print 'bar:', bar
return printed
```

which produces the following output on my US/Eastern machine:

```
foo: 2016/06/15 07:00:00 GMT+0
bar: 2016/06/15 07:00:00 GMT-4
```

This idiosyncratic behavior is intentional, although the original cause for it is lost in the mists of time.

After thinking about this a bit, I believe that the approach used in that PR (explicitly passing client instances in resource constructors) is a good idea, at least as a first step towards refactoring client configuration.

The only externally visible change would be when instantiating resources "manually" (as mentioned by @jonprindiville). For example, it is now possible to create a Customer like this:

```python
c = Customer(data)
c.save()
```

This code snippet will need to become the following, which feels a little weird (why does the resource need to know about the client?):

```python
c = Customer(data, client=client)
c.save()
```

However, it could be argued that a more "normal" way to create the same customer would be through the client itself (which wouldn't be changing):

```python
c = client.customers.create(data)
```

The signatures of `Resource.{save,_create,_update}` wouldn't need to be modified to accept a `client` argument, since it is guaranteed that `Resource._client` is set at initialization time.

I believe that the only place resources are manually instantiated is in unit tests.

regarding a):

This is not how it works. Travis can never know the value of the OANDA_API_TOKEN. If you use it from your shell this works. But the shell Travis uses has the value not set, so it it empty.

If you want to use the/your token in the tests (and I would advise you not to) you need to set it up the way Travis points out in their docs, using encryption. The owner of the repo must do this since it involves the a procedure with keys.
You end up with a section in .travis looking like:
```yaml:
env:
  global:
  - secure: xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
```
But when you do it this way you share a token as a contributor to the repo-owner. You do not want that for both parties:
- as a contributor you can revoke the token and tests would break, so the owner relies on a contributor here
- As owner you have access to a token which was offered on certain conditions by the issueing party

If you want to use the token in the tests this way I suggest the best solution is that the repo-owner obtains a token and does the Travis setup with that token. This way the owner has control and contributors can still run the tests.

If you want to use encrypted environment variables, it is up to to repo-owner to set this up.

I use this encryption setup in https://github.com/hootnot/oanda-api-v20/ to deploy to pypi automatically when tagging a release.

I still think that you should use *requests_mock* to setup your tests

> It turns out, that gzip is little more than twice as fast as brotli, when compressing this HTML.
> This fails may expectations - I thought the Brotli outnumbers gzip.

Brotli is generally slower than gzip for compression.  It is usually a bit faster at **de**compression, and the compression ratio is much higher.  See https://quixdb.github.io/squash-benchmark/?dataset=cp.html&machine=peltast&visible-plugins=brotli,zlib#ratio-vs-decompression for results for cp.html; results are probably similar for most datasets.  If you turn on some of the other codecs squash offers (click the label in the legend) you'll get a more complete picture of where brotli fits in with the competition.

This is a pretty good place for brotli to be.  Compressing content once and decompressing many times is a pretty common use case, so it's usually better to emphasize decompressor speed and ratio at the expense of compressor speed.

Yeah, this will definitely be a bit tricky for raven-python to handle, since by nature, there's no way we can try to do this in-process, since this state is already in chaos, this is why faulthandler is written in C to begin with and it allocates a specific amount of memory to work outside the normal failing world.

I think the best possible thing we could do is as you mentioned, some sort of post mortem, read the crash log written to disk.

But unfortunately, we have nothing that works like this today, so it'll require some thought on how we'd best want to handle this.

@mitsuhiko might have some better thoughts on this. What's proposed seems the only sane way IMO. The alternative is we have a second binary that's shipped, like, `raven-faulthandler` whose job is to read the file output and report. That means your app isn't responsible to send the report when it starts back up. Maybe it's signature is just `raven-faulthandler [DSN] [FILE]`, so it reads the crash, then sends it along as a separate explicit action?

I'd be sorta -1 on doing it on boot with `raven-python` automatically since for whatever reason, that may make your application slower to start up, or even crash, etc. And I definitely wouldn't want that.

  - VisServer integration: 
        - VisServer makes a request to aggregate data, requests contains location of data to aggregate and aggregation columns (technically i suggested to call `System.exec` for first integration, later we can think about JNI integration)

The problem:
   - VisServer triggers multiple aggregation for different data columns. 
Solution: 
    (A) We can solve parallelization of multiple requests on Java side, which at the end will mean that we trigger `datatable.aggregator` multiple times (we will pay penalty for process fork + multiple mmaps per datatable invocation), OR
    (B) We will solve parallelization directly in datatable.aggregate (the same `dt` as input, but different `options`)  - for example, the method can compute multiple aggregation in single pass (following @st-pasha  proposal)

One example might be a `created_by` field which is auto-populated by the server (on creation) with the authenticated user (e.g. OAuth `uid` scope), as a user I want to be able to retrieve such resource, modify only known fields (the ones I care for) and `PUT` the resource again without additionally stripping `readOnly` fields.

connexion just manage the open api specification layer, there's no definitions about the data pagination on open api specification. So, you can define your api pagination like it:

```yml
paths:
  /my-data:
    get:
      opertionId: myPackage.get_my_data
      parameters:
        - name: page
           in: query
           type: integer
           default: 1
```

but all the pagination logic will be implemented by you.

@hjacobs flask.Response is handled by Produces(BaseSerializer) class:
```python
class Produces(BaseSerializer):
    def __call__(self, function):
        """
        :type function: types.FunctionType
        :rtype: types.FunctionType
        """

        @functools.wraps(function)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            url = flask.request.url
            data, status_code, headers = self.get_full_response(function(*args, **kwargs))
            logger.debug('Returning %s', url, extra={'url': url, 'mimetype': self.mimetype})
            if isinstance(data, flask.Response):  # if the function returns a Response object don't change it
                logger.debug('Endpoint returned a Flask Response', extra={'url': url, 'mimetype': data.mimetype})
                return data

Just figured out, this is due to the special batch implementation in DyNet. In case that other beginners may wonder, let me explain a little.

You simply can't transpose the batch dim of an expression. A batched matrix is actually a list of vectors, so dy.transpose only transpose those column vectors (100,) into row vectors (1, 100). It can be reproduced like this:

```
import dynet as dy
import numpy as np

a = np.eye(100, 149)
b = dy.inputTensor(a, batched=True)
print(dy.transpose(b).npvalue().shape)
```

Outputs:

```
(1, 100, 149)
```

Several comments about the biomass equation.

Real DCW contains ash content and organic matter. Most biomass equations only have organic matter even though I have seen ash content ranging from 5-10% in _S. cerevisiae_. If the biomass equation doesn't have ash content then I think its fine to have a biomass equation that is between 0.9 and 1 gDCW.

There are two basic ways of specifying the biomass composition. A lumped reaction with all biomass precursors (most published models follow this format), and breaking the biomass equation into several reactions. I think more "points" should be given to the latter since it is easier to read and modify.

**Lumped biomass reaction.**
Not everyone uses the correct molar masses to balance the biomass equation but I'd expect it to become more clear with memote. I have seen people use the full molar mass to balance the biomass equation.  The "dehydrated molar mass" should be used for the amino acids and carbohydrates. And (MM of XTP - MM ppi) for  RNA/DNA.

**Segregated biomass reaction.** 
I actually have some code I wrote in python to build these reactions given some inputs which I can share.
- Sum(vi AAi) + x H (required for neutral protein compound) -> [Sum(vi) -1 ] H2O1 + 1 gDCW protein (neutral charge). Input is mol AA / mol protein, which is what can be found experimentally.
- Sum(vi XTP) + Sum(vi) H -> Sum(vi) ppi + H2O + 1 gDCW RNA. I calculated from gene content.
- Sum(vi dXTP) + Sum(vi) H -> Sum(vi) ppi + H2O + 1 gDCW DNA. Calculated from genome GC content.
(additional constrain that mol A = mol T, mol C=molG)
I also use reactions for single species to convert them into 1 gram species below since they can change depending on the organism or conditions:
-4.9208 chitin -> chitin_1g, glycogen, trehalose.

Many phospholipid reactions are quite repeitive or cumbersome to modify. Different versions I have seen include:
**iMM904**
0.02 dcacoa[c] + 0.06 ddcacoa[c] + glyc3p[c] + 0.17 hdcoa[c] + 0.09 ocdycacoa[c] + 0.24 odecoa[c] + 0.27 pmtcoa[c] + 0.05 stcoa[c] + 0.1 tdcoa[c]  -> 0.01 1ag3p_SC[c] + coa[c] 
**YMN6.05**
1-acyl-sn-glycerol 3-phosphate [endoplasmic reticulum] + acyl-CoA [endoplasmic reticulum]  <=> coenzyme A [endoplasmic reticulum] + 4 H+ [endoplasmic reticulum] + phosphatidate [endoplasmic reticulum] (similar to what I have seen in Pathway Tools)
**YMN 7.6.1**
1-acyl-sn-glycerol 3-phosphate (18:1) [Golgi membrane] + H2O [Golgi membrane] -> 1-monoglyceride (18:1) [Golgi membrane] + phosphate [Golgi membrane]

My preference is having a combination of iMM904 and YMN6.05 where I specifiy a composition for the acyl-coa, which then feeds into reactions that consume it. The phospholipid molecules then create the DW phospholipid.

- 0.0115 tdcoa[c] + 0.1533 pmtcoa[c] + 0.0184 stcoa[c] + 0.5665 hdcoa[c] + 0.2504 odecoa[c] -> acoa[c] (monounsaturated)
- 0.1482 pmtcoa[c] + 0.2347 hdcoa[c] + 0.3265 odecoa[c] + 0.2225 lnlccoa[c] + 0.0682 lnlncgcoa[c] -> acoa[c] (polyunsaturated)
- 0.0791 tdcoa[c] + 0.7941 pmtcoa[c] + 0.1268 stcoa[c] -> acoa[c] (saturated)

-pc+pa+ps+pg+pe+ptd1ino+clpn -> phospholipid_1g

The advantage of this method is that calculating the molar mass of the phospholipid compounds is easier (for 1 gDCW test), as is modifying the acyl-coa composition.  Some databases do not have chemical information for these molecules (or did not last time I did model curation).

All of these reactions can then feed into the biomass equation which can be easily edited (assuming composition of amino acids in protein and and XTP's in RNA are contstant). Another advantage of this segregated biomass equation is that it is easier to debug which macromolecule cannot be synthesized. It's an awful feeling to load a model and see no growth, but with the segregated biomass equation you can set each macromolecule as the objective function to see where the gap is.

a (1 gDW ash) + b (1 gDW phospholipids) + c (free fatty acids)+ d (1 gDW carbs) +  e (1 gDW protein) + f (1 gDW RNA) + g (1 gDW DNA) + h (vitamins/cofactors)-> 1 gDCW biomass
where a+b+c+d+e+f+g+h=1.

I realize this is more complex but it's worth the effort in my opinion.

I would:
1. Check for GPR Rule
2. Check for EC-Number
Potentially:
Check other annotations (e.g. a Uniprot link, would indicate enzymatic reaction).

3. Check SBO Term - With additional requested Terms.

If all reactions are defined or clearly associated: Succeed, otherwise fail that quality test.

If I get your intention, your tool should serve as a quality check for models. As such you can set some standards, and I think it makes sense to have reaction types associated/properly annotated.
And now the but:
SBO Terms are very often (not in the Cobra toolbox were its just unassigned by default, but I have seen this in multiple models) set to a specific "basic" Term, without that term being correct. So a model that passes this test only informs, that the type is set, not that it makes sense (which is problematic imo).
What I would, in general suggest:
If a model has no GPR rule, but an SBO that says Enzyme-catalysed, than something is off. An SBO "unknown mechanism" would be fine, even "enzymatic with unknown enzyme", makes sense, but if you check these things, make sure, that the different fields are consistent.
e.g. 
No GPR -> Should not be enzymatic, or at least indicating SBO:unknown Enzyme
SBO:Unknown Enzyme -> not allowed to have a GPR (otherwise people will use unknown Enzyme as default SBO).
etc...

From profiling, I believe I have a handle on why we see a performance regression: because `Shared` has its own internal mutex (with the memory costs that that entails), it was able to be operated on outside of the `Graph`'s mutex. But as implemented, this patch removes all mutexes other than the `Graph` mutex, and so we end up spending more time under that lock.

There are two concrete improvements possible:
1) Part of what happens under the `Graph` mutex is the cloning of `Value`s to send to the `waiters` of a `Running->Completed` transitioning entry. By switching the `NodeResult::Value` type to wrap an `Arc` we could avoid calling into python while under the lock (or outside of it, for that matter!)
2) Rather than executing `fn get` under the lock, we could wrap the `state` field an `Arc<Atomic<..>>` (from the `crossbeam` crate). This would allow us to rapidly clone the state field while under the lock, and then operate on it atomically outside of any mutex. It's also possible that ``
