Ok, here are some ideas on how this would work, working from the outside in:

* `Document` has a `start_bookmark()` method. This is what you call as a developer using `python-docx`.

* The bookmark is actually added in the `w:body` element, so `Document.start_bookmark()` delegates that job to its `_Body` object by calling the single line `return self._body.start_bookmark()`. You see this same pattern with other API methods of `Document` like `.add_paragraph()` etc.

* `_Body` gets its `.start_bookmark()` method by inheriting it from `BlockItemContainer`. Its `._element` attribute gets set to its `w:body` element in the `super(_Body, self).__init__(body_elm, ...)` call in its constructor (__init__ method). This is because the `._element` attribute is actually provided by `BlockItemContainer` and `_Body` inherits it.

* So the error you should get, if any, once all this is wired correctly, is `CT_Body` object (or whatever the body element is called) has no attribute `add_bookmarkStart`. This is remedied by adding the `bookmarkStart = ZeroOrMore(...)` line to the `CT_Body` class.

> Cat codes transformation is able to deal with new values while predicting

Could you tell me more about this fabulous "Cat codes" transformation? Which Python class, in which Python package? Perhaps it would be appropriate to incorporate it into JPMML-SkLearn and SkLearn2PMML projects.

> Can you suggest a way how to deal with the PMML with the encoding, either in python or in JPMML side.

When dealing with categorical features, then the first transformation of a pipeline should be `sklearn2pmml.decoration.CategoricalDomain`, which lets you customize missing value and invalid value treatments according to the PMML specification.

You could try the following parametrizations:

- `CategoricalDomain(invalid_value_treatment = "as_is")` - Invalid values are dispatched to the model. It is assumed that the model contains logic for detecting and handling these.
- `CategoricalDomain(invalid_value_treatment = "as_missing")` - Invalid values are converted to missing values, and then dispatched to the model.
- `CategoricalDomain(invalid_value_treatment = "as_missing", missing_value_replacement = "1.0")`. Convert invalid values to missing values, and then perform missing value replacement. The total effect is that the model sees invalid values as "1.0".

This isn't a bug. This is a result of the terrible inconsistency in the ElementTree API about whether an `Element` gets returned or an `ElementTree` after parsing. We return an `ElementTree`, which has a single root element and one cannot append to it. `etree.fromstring` returns an `Element` which is just a normal element (and `etree.parse` returns an `ElementTree`, to prove it isn't consistent).

See `ElementTree.getroot()` to get the root `Element` of a tree.

I believe fontmake/glyphsLib already support outputting instances with custom `familyName`.

fontmake by default only generates instances that have the same `familyName` as the master font. However it has an option `--family-name`, described in the `--help` as:

> Family name to use for masters, and to filter output instances.

This string is passed on to [`glyphsLib.build_masters`](https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/blob/73c50f91a1a71c65193dd4925bdcbc62ce2caa02/Lib/glyphsLib/__init__.py#L81-L82), and is used to name the master UFOs and select which instances are to be generated. I no --family-name is provided, the master font `familyName` is used for this, thus excluding instances which may have different custom parameter.

PuLP only has three types of constraints: LpConstraintEqual, LpConstraintGE and LpConstraintLE(for ==, >= and <=, respectively).
The reasoning for this is that it can also deal with floating point values, and giving you the "next" floating point for a > constraint is not really nice(and a lot of the underlying solvers don't implement that kind of constraint for this reason).
The fact that you didn't get an error using > instead of >= is something that could(should?) probably be fixed.

Keep in mind that traffic bound for the builder is actually proxied through the routers.  Even when cross-zone load-balancing isn't on, requests bound for the builder may arrive at _any_ of the routers and may then cross zones from there if the router it hits isn't in the same zone as the builder-- which will be likely the majority of the time if you're running 3+ routers to the one builder.  Enabling cross zone load balancing shouldn't affect that equation.

@MaxBenChrist further to this here ``n_jobs=64`` is being used, having looked
into the next steps in the code, I believe that the new ``data_in_chucks`` list
which is going to be very big, is being passed via ``MultiprocessingDistributor``
to ``distributor.map_reduce(_do_extraction_on_chunk, data=data_in_chunks``

At 350000000 rows / 64 workers that is a chunk_size of 5468750 rows
Although the data representation of id/kind is probably less than the raw data
this is still a LOT of list data.

I think the issue is there seem to be too many duplications of the data.

When generating all the chunk lists in the staticmethod ``partition`` that at
this point tsfresh has:

- original ``timeseries_container`` dataframe object passed to ``extract_features``
- the ``df_melt`` dataframe object (basically a copy of the ``timeseries_container``) created with ``_normalize_input_to_internal_representation``
- The ``data_in_chunks`` list object (another duplication of the data in a different representation)
- And the ``partition`` generator object (I am not certain how big this object is yet, but it seems to increment and decrement objects with each worker)
- And 64 partition chunk list objects (basically another duplication of the data in a different representation and in 64 pieces)

So the data in the process at the point that the distributor = MultiprocessingDistributor
is running on 64 workers, the original is data multiplied by ~4 (to keep it simple)
with the parent process having 3 representations concurrently as there is no
cleaning up of any of the objects after they have been used as far as I can
determine and timeseries_container dataframe object,  df_melt dataframe object
and data_in_chunks list object exist for the duration of the run.

I am still going through all this step by step through the code and verifying
but this is how it is looking at the moment. I think that ``del timeseries_container``
as soon as the ``df_melt`` Dataframe has been created
via ``_normalize_input_to_internal_representation`` would at least reduce memory
by ~30% as a start as I can see ``timeseries_container`` being used thereafter.

I'm not sure this is an issue with Conda.  If it is, then it is a major
design flaw with the whole "Conda rides on the back of the root env"
concept.  I don't see any way around this: yes, if you have processes
running that are using files, then you won't be able to replace/remove
those files.  The only way around this is to have those files not in use,
which means two Python installations - one for conda, and another for the
long-running process.

For Anaconda Server or whatever, this can be just two Miniconda
installations - one hosting the long-running process, and another for the
update stuff.

Are there generally programs that are happy having their host program
replaced out from under them?  I guess Linux continues with whatever is

Here is the problem, at least with version "2016-02-03" of the API (which is what azure-mgmt-iothub 0.2.2 uses), it is returning the "rights" property value in a different order than [specified](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/python/api/azure.mgmt.iothub.models.accessrights?view=azure-python). It is being returned as "ServiceConnect, DeviceConnect, RegistryWrite"  **instead of** "RegistryWrite, ServiceConnect, DeviceConnect" which ties to the registry_write_service_connect_device_connect enum member hence the exception message:

'ServiceConnect, DeviceConnect, RegistryWrite' is not valid value for enum <enum 'AccessRights'>

Not an Az CLI issue but an azure mgmt API one as far as I can tell.

In AWS, we have a configuration for each major version which references `openaddr/machine:5.x`, `openaddr/machine:6.x`, and so on. I don’t want those to reference full major/minor/patch versions because the updates to launch configurations would be a pain on each release. Major version upgrades often involve a short period of overlap while I bring up new versions, check that they work, and then spin down old ones. If I need to back up or slow down an upgrade, using `:latest` is going to be weird. This has happened a couple times.

It sounds like the suggestion here should be: publish `openaddr/machine:latest` for the benefit of users, but stick with major version tags for the deployed configurations. Also, borrow Python’s tag conventions and switch to e.g. `openaddr/machine:6` instead of the confusing `6.x`.

Ah this is probably because these hooks also use `types` and have `types: [python]`.  `types` and `files` are an `AND` operation (that is, `files: \.eb$` will only further filter the files matched by `types: [python]`).

In this case, you'll want to override the base `types` back to all files and then filter to files matching `.eb` -- something like this:

```yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    sha: v1.1.1
    hooks:
      - id: autopep8-wrapper
        types: [file]
        files: \.eb$
      - id: check-ast
        types: [file]
        files: \.eb$
      # you'll probably also want to lint python files as well
      - id: autopep8-wrapper
      - id: check-ast
```

pre-commit allows you to specify multiple configurations of the same hook.  If you want to differentiate them you can even override name:

```yaml
      - id: autopep8-wrapper
        name: 'autopep8-wrapper (.eb)'
        types: [file]
        files: \.eb$
      - id: autopep8-wrapper
```

You'll get something like this:

```
autopep8 wrapper (.eb)......................................................Passed
autopep8 wrapper............................................................Passed
```

> Maybe it would be useful to have a wrapper function that waits on a bunch of tasks and then checks that they all succeeded?

That's pretty trivial IIUC:

```
In [7]: async def wait_and_use(tasks):
   ...:     await asyncio.wait(tasks)
   ...:     return [t.result() for t in tasks]
   ...:
```

But it feel like the usage of `asyncio.wait(tasks)` is unnatural, usually you should have a consumer of the task. You will rarely directly ask AsyncIO to to wait for it. 

I don't know if there would be a way to detect earlier that a task has no potential consumers without triggering a gc collection. 

The current behavior seem totally natural and expected though, as you still have tasks.
I **guess**, we could try to count the number of reference for all tasks linked from `__main__` namespace and print a warning if there is no other consumer, but that seem like over engineering  things for (little) benefits.

> If we change later, more notebooks will break

The flip side is that when you change something that's going to break some things, it's much easier if there's a compelling advantage that comes with the changes. The 'eat your vegetables' approach - do this because it's the right thing to do, doesn't make us many friends.

 * "Run this command (which could be easily discovered via `peru help` -> `peru module help` docs?), we'll write the `imports:` & `module` boilerplate YAML for you."

Peru already writes to its configuration file with `peru reup`... so some of the plumbing for these capabilities are already in place, right? And `peru override` has some basic CRUD as well.

It looks like the playable area of the map is 64x64, but that actual size of the map is 96x88. The feature layers are currently rendered in terms of the actual map size. The camera size of 24 doesn't divide nicely into 88, which is probably why moving the camera doesn't seem smooth.

We could change the renderer to use playable area if we think that would be better.

Note that this isn't fixable with just changing the map. The engine requires there to be a ~7 unit gap outside the playable area so there is something to render when you have your camera up against the edge of the map.

I think a solution that perfectly handles all corner cases with the behavior I described is likely to be extremely convoluted. Perhaps, I should simplify it and just make it a best effort approach. We could try to call each function once and if it is interrupted, the function is not called again.

This would involve a `pop` before calling the function and has the negative side effect that if a signal happens right between the `pop` and the call, the function is never called at all. While unfortunate, it is simpler, will never call a function multiple times, and is likely to be "good enough" in most cases.

I agree we need better documentation for how to automate everything using the command line. To answer your specific questions:

>Do you want to renew and replace this certificate with a newly-issued one?

If you want to duplicate the behavior you got here, provide `--duplicate`, but this probably isn't what you want. This will create a new directory in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/` for every run of the client. Instead, what you probably want is `--renew-by-default` which will just update the current directory.

>Which virtual host would you like to choose?

There's actually not a way to answer this question on the command line at this time. However, if you add `ServerName` or `ServerAlias` directives to your virtual host, the Apache plugin can determine which virtual host needs to be modified and will not ask this question.

>Please choose whether HTTPS access is required or optional.

Use `--redirect`.

        msg = consumer.poll()
        if not msg.error():
            print("Good Message:")
            print("    Topic: " + str(msg.topic()))
            print("    Partition: " + str(msg.partition()))
            print("    Offset: " + str(msg.offset()))
            pass
        else:
            if msg.error().code() == KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
                print("Error Message:")
                print("    Topic: " + str(msg.topic()))
                print("    Partition: " + str(msg.partition()))
                print("    Offset: " + str(msg.offset()))
                print("    Error: " + str(msg.error()))
                running = False
            else:
                print(msg.error())
                running = False

@izverev 
This session does not have any other updates than `UpdateUserPhoto`, and in this case response is `Difference`, not `DifferenceSlice`, since I'm trying to get difference right after updating state manually.

Though I do have problems with `DifferenceSlice` for some other sessions (mostly those whose updates haven't been handled before).
 In this case I'm using new intermediate state from a `DifferenceSlice` as an input state for the next iteration of `GetDifferenceRequest`, but all incoming differences contain the same state parameters as before with exception of `date`, thus loop stucks.

You can avoid infinite loop of `DifferenceSlice` by replacing current session state with the most recent from servers by `GetStateRequest`, but all intermediate updates will be skipped. At least this allows you to proceed to further updates.

@Lonami 
I've also found some minor bugs in code.
1) Some updates has attribute `pts` with value `None`. This raises an exception when trying to handle such updates in this part (`telethon\client\updates.py`):
``` python
if hasattr(update, 'pts'):
    if self._state.pts and (update.pts - self._state.pts) > 1:
        need_diff = True
```
Better use
``` python
if getattr(update, `pts`, None):
```
2) Some inconsistencies with a timestamp attribute inside `State`. Due to UTC convertation timestamp inside a session file is not the same as returned by `get_update_state(0)`. You can check it by yourself.
``` python
print(state.date)
client.session.set_update_state(0, state)
print(client.session.get_update_state(0).date)
```
This will give you two different dates.

UserSchema(unknown=ALLOW)

I found a solution to this that allowed me to avoid using custom fields (I'm using apispec and want to minimize that where possible). I added a hook:

```python
class SelfReferentialSchema(Schema):
    @pre_dump
    def add_self_reference(self, data):
        setattr(data, "_marshmallow_self_reference", data)
```

Then you can refer to this on the `fields.Nested` call:

```python
class UserSchema(SelfReferentialSchema):
    name = fields.String()
    meta = fields.Nested(MetaSchema(), attribute="_marshmallow_self_reference")
```

Unfortunately, there's no clean way that I can see to remove this; `@post_dump` doesn't receive the original data.

It looks like you've stumbled on a pretty common limitation of pickle. There is no way to pickle a multiprocessing pipe because it depends on an active external resource: the other end of the pipe. cloudpickle is mainly used in a distributed context, where the receiver of an object may be on another machine. In this case, a multiprocessing pipe would be unable to establish communication back to the original machine, so we would not be able to reconstruct this object. This can still be a challenge if you are on the same machine, for example: if I send the the pipe object to another process, it will not have access to the underlying operating system pipe to communicate. Another problem would be if I save the pickled Pipe object to disk and then read it back later. In this case, the process I am trying to communicate with may have already exited or closed the pipe. This is very similar to trying to pickle an open file. If I saved a file object and sent it to another machine, it would not be able to recreate the object because the file may not be available on that new machine. Hopefully this helps explain why cloudpickle (or pickle) cannot save multiprocessing pipes.

Hi, 
is there a reason why you need `main` and `train` outside of sacred? Because if not, you could just make `main` your sacred main function and then pass the dataset around normally. I think your code could become a lot simpler that way. 

If you really need them on the outside, you could run the experiment manually. So in your `train` function, you would:
```python
def train(args, params):
    dataset, params = prepare(args, params)
    config(params) 
    run = ex._create_run()
    run(dataset)
```
This would pass dataset to the `sacred_main` function.

The reason is simply that the information that you might want to store alongside your experiment can differ in structure. A schemaless database allows to just store it anyways and MongoDB even provides a powerful query-engine. 

That being said, SQL would be nice to have since it is the most widespread and well-developed database technology. If you are interested, I have some work-in-progress in the `feature/sql` branch.
Unfortunately I don't have any time at the moment to work on this.

The formulation is a non-linear expression over finite domain variables. 
Z3 does not fully support non-linear optimization and in this case determines that it cannot support this constraint. 
You can turn it into a linear function by distributing over multiplication. E.g.,

Chiming in: [angr-z3](https://github.com/angr/angr-z3) has the necessary changes to properly install Z3 on OSX for Python 2.7.11 and Python 3.5.1. It is also pip-installable on Linux. Some Windows support is there already and it should work out of the box, however it is untested (I also do not have a Windows box to test).

One of the core reasons why I prefer that a Python package installs its own library: I can take a virtualenv, freeze it, and install it on a different machine and I *know* that it works. This is a common task and makes reproducibility trivial. However, using system-wide Z3 forces me to make sure that the Z3 Python API I'm installing actually can use the library that I have installed system-wide and that there is no feature that I am calling from Python code that the (older) system-wide library does not support (in fact, Ubuntu and Debian both only provide 4.4.0, which contains a "multitude of bugs"). In addition, I now have to install system-wide packages on top of the Python ones, or I have manually check the library version and install it into the virtualenv. The former is something that -as a normal user- I might not be allowed to do. The latter is ugly and cumbersome. Hence, I strongly advocate that the Python API installs its own version of the library (which would only be used in Python virtualenv).

Overall, personally, I would go with option 3 as it the intended and practically only supported way and because it has a majority of benefits over the other "hacky" solutions that we are discussing (see zardus' and asmeurer's (sdist setup.py in root) arguments).

Out of curiosity, without wanting to step on any toes: Is the plan to support almost all package managers for all languages that have an API or just the main one? What other (main) package managers require files in the root directory? How many files would end up in the root directory at most? Is this maybe less of a problem than we think it is? What would prevent it from being moved in sub-directory at a later point (if the root really becomes clobbered or when better support for this is available in pip), but so that it is easily pip-installable now? Assuming that installs on Windows work, wouldn't this solve the entire issue?

In this particular case, the easiest way to get rid of `bvsdiv_i` by replacing it by and if-then-else that checks whether the divisor is non-zero (in which case `bvsdiv` works fine), or else returns an uninterpreted constant.

This is not a bug. The tactic qfnra does not understand how to handle integers. It only knows the quantifier-free theory of reals. The default strategy invoked by check-sat uses a general purpose solver, which is not tuned for non-linear reals, but happens to do just the necessary bounds propagations to understand that there is no integer square root of 2.

This is a frequently reported issue; the problem is that the first occurrence of a push/pop makes Z3 switch to a completely different solver that supports incrementality. Before that, Z3 assumes that it won't need those features and it can thus run different, non-incremental algorithms. If you need the incremental features, you can add a (push) into every file you emit, thus you will always experience the performance of the incremental solver during development. Obviously, if you don't need those features, don't use them :-)

becomes (roughly):

Yes, thats nice catch. I've always been working with 32bit binaries, so never thought about 64bit.

I wasn't able to get angr-management running here but I haven't put a lot of effort into it so maybe I'll give that another try. I agree that only focusing on a single binary is most limiting; expanding that across multiple binaries would be a huge leg up over IDA. I'll take a look at nightmaRE to see what's already in there too.

As far as IDA goes, it's a great tool, especially with IDAPython. While IDA's internal analysis isn't something I could really duplicate or improve upon, I think a lot of the same information could be exposed, to the extent that angr/CLE have the fundamental analysis done. 

Some things I can do with IDA that could be duplicated by angr/CLE:

* Assign pseudo-symbols to functions and strings (allows renaming, bringing along the "interactive" from IDA.
* I've got an IDAPython script that I use to identify all jump tables, or just a jump table at some address, and spit out the addresses for each target location. I then just regex this into a Python list to make a nice 'avoid' list for `simgr` once I've identified the few cases I care about it.
* Identify symbol cross-references (probably the hardest thing in this list)
* Signature scanning based on [open-source FLIRT signatures](https://github.com/Maktm/FLIRTDB)
* Simplified disassembly (e.g., `p.get_symbol('somefunc').showdisasm(flavor='intel')`)

To do most of this work cross-platform, the API for ELF and PE backends needs to be better standardized. I'm refactoring PE and WinReloc now, so I can get standard information from ELF, MetaELF, PE and Relocation but I can tell everything was mostly written with ELF in mind so there's a lot that would need to be abstracted out to the base Backend, Section and Relocation classes.

Anyway, just my $0.02. I know yet another major refactor may not be something you guys want to take on at the moment so, for now, I'm keeping away from altering the base classes and just expanding/improving upon PE and WinReloc, to mirror the implementations from the ELF side. I've already added all the PE relocations to archinfo and new arch modules for relocations (named "peamd64", "pearm", etc)

Once I work out some kinks in the ASCII table printing, I'll put in a PR for CLE to at least get some review on the static analysis pieces and figure out exactly how to name it or expose the API. I'll do  separate PRs for the Windows stuff.

I am always open to better suggestions :-)

I'd be happy if we can avoid an import-time side effect.  I'm always up for more dependency injection, and while a context object that encapsulates the scheme registry and that users can definitely avoids mutating global state, it presents its own design challenges:

1. `register_scheme` adds schemes to `hyperlink`, whereas ashes' contexts share nothing with default behavior, except perhaps through inheritance.  A `hyperlink` context object would have to either chain itself to existing context objects or allow itself to be cloned and then mutated.  That's no less like prototypes than returning a `URL` factory that knows about new schemes, so if one is unidiomatic so is the other.
2. The various methods that create `hyperlink.URL` instances  -- `URL.__init__`, `URL.from_text`, and `URL.replace` -- would have to take a new argument.  They already take a lot of arguments.  This isn't a huge deal and will be less of a deal if closing #24 leads to `Scheme` objects.

"Inputs are high impedance, 1.8V CMOS voltage levels.  However, it is important to make sure to use a high resolution signal generator.  The frequency of the signal generator needs to exactly match the sensor frame rate.  For example, if the sensor is set up as 30 fps, the real frame rate may be 30.015 fps.  You may need to use an oscilloscope to measure the real frame and configure the signal generator to the same frequency.  For this reason, it may be better to just using one additional camera as master sync signal generator"

Hi @specter119 

Thanks for submitting a PR, but there are a couple of issues here which is why I am closing the PR.

1 - For line length in the codebases I manage (fireworks, matminer, atomate), I would like to set 100 characters as a soft limit for line length and 120 of a more hard limit. The PEP guideline of 80 in my experience reduces code readability for lines in the 80-100 character range much more often than it helps. I agree with @bocklund that the specific changes here actually serve to reduce readability.

2 - The code for SLURM cancellation, etc., would have been fine in MPWorks, but the reason we created the new code atomate was to keep the workflow definitions as clean as possible. This means that in atomate, it is not allowed to mix commands pertaining to specific queuing systems, etc. within the workflow definitions. The workflows are all about materials science procedure and not about details of killing jobs properly. For the latter, you can either try to do this via custodian. Either write a handler and pass it in to the atomate workflows, or write a new custodian Job and pass that in to atomate. However, the Firetasks, Fireworks, and Workflow definitions should be as minimal as possible and directly related to the essentials of the calculation procedure.

Again, thanks for trying this, but we are doing some policy changes for atomate vs. MPWorks.

Pull request should be short (we discussed this during our meeting and you also mentioned a 200 line guideline at your Google internship). You should keep just the level 1 pull request and only submit a PR for the subsequent ones when that one is merged, etc. the multiple simultaneous and overlapping PRs are definitely not working so well

My understanding is that `.only()` is in fact a performance hit in many cases. This may seem counter intuitive, but it comes down the the fact that the returned model object from an only query is much more complex than a raw one, and this extra time is a bigger hit that the extra work your database does.

See [the documentation](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/models/querysets/#defer) for only and defer, the note pretty much says "don't do this unless you absolutely have to".

Oh. I found a solution, much simpler, in case of you are interested. 

I defined a decorator for the queries in all apps:
```python
class graphql_subquery:
    """Class decorator to include a class in the global GraphQL query"""
    query_classes = []

    def __init__(self, cls):
        self.cls = cls
        graphql_subquery.query_classes.append(cls)

    def __call__(self):
        self.cls()
```
And then in the project's schema.py, collect the queries from the decorator class, and create the main `Query` class dynamically:
```python
bases = tuple(graphql_subquery.query_classes + [graphene.ObjectType, object])
for cls in bases:
    print("Including '{}' in global GraphQL Query...".format(cls.__name__))

Query = type('Query', bases, {})
```
This works perfectly here.

Another small problem is that in `resolve_related` you need to translate the field names from GraphQL (camel case) to SQLAlchemy (snake case) with `field = to_snake_case(field)`, otherwise it doesn't work with table columns with underscores in them. You can import the `to_snake_case` method from `graphene.utils.str_converters`.

I feel something like this should be really integrated into graphene-sqlalchemy. This advantage would be greater if it existed also on the level of the database, not only on the network level.

@camilonova - please re-open this issue as the concerns raised have still not been fully addressed.

1. The default configuration of `django-ipware` is unsafe due to the full range of headers specified in `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER`.
2. The lack of documentation in `django-axes` to warn that `django-ipware` and any proxy in front of `django-axes` need to be configured correctly.

What @SunilMohanAdapa has called a "workaround" is in fact what must be done to correctly and safely configure the proxy headers.

Putting this plainly, using a real example, `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` must not contain `HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR` if nginx does not do the following:

```nginx
proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
```

Also note that this must be `proxy_set_header` and not `proxy_pass_header`. Without correct configuration, any client can set anything in the header bypassing any of the protection that `django-axes` claims to provide.

Obviously, `django-ipware` ought to set a safe default for `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER`, using values such as `REMOTE_ADDR` which are usually automatically explicitly set by the server, but that ship has sailed. Ideally it should force you to specify the one header that you expect, and have none by default raising [`ImproperlyConfigured`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/ref/exceptions/#improperlyconfigured). (Ping @un33k)

I have submitted #1962 to support multiple microseconds timer, it adds 320 bytes flash and 32bytes RAM.

I made some changes to the previous solution, and follow @abtink's suggestion to add separate `UsecTimer` and `UsecTimerScheduler`, because of that:

Since #1926, to reduce RAM usage in single Instance mode, the `TimerSchedulerLocator` does not store the `TimerScheduler` initialized in Timer's constructor, which makes it difficult to reuse `class Timer` for both Msec and Usec Timer without adding the `mScheduler` back (will add 112 bytes RAM).

Another possible solution I proposed in #1954 is adding a parameter `aTimerScheduler` to `Timer::Start()` and `Timer::Stop()` function, but the change will add ~1k bytes flash. So finally I think that adding separate `UsecTimer` and `UsecTimerScheduler` is the most efficient way.

@isalirezag `win` is the unique name of a window inside an environment. It is a string. In the first snippet, the string `win` will be generated automatically by the library, so in case you want to update the window, you'll need to reuse it as ` vis.scatter(..., win=win)`. You can also use this to manually set the name of your window.

This is my current workaround, which seems to work fine for my needs:
```python
def vplot(Y, X, title, xlabel, ylabel, name, win):
    msg = vis.updateTrace(Y=Y, X=X, name=name, win=win)

    if msg == 'win does not exist':
       options = dict(title=title ,  xlabel=xlabel,  ylabel=ylabel,  legend=[name])
       vis.line(X=X, Y=Y ,opts=options, win=win) 
```

Hi @oal17 

The current wrapper we have for `line` indeed does not support that, but you can totally just construct it yourself following the plotly convention:

https://plot.ly/python/line-charts/
https://plot.ly/python/reference/ (you can find the supported symbols there)

A simple example:

```
import visdom
vis = visdom.Visdom()

trace = dict(x=[1,2,3], y=[4,5,6], marker={'color': 'red', 'symbol': 104, 'size': "10"},
                    mode="markers+lines",  text=["one","two","three"], name='1st Trace')
layout=dict(title="First Plot", xaxis={'title':'x1'}, yaxis={'title':'x2'})

vis._send(data=[trace], layout=layout, win='mywin')
```

Hope that helps!
You can also try `python setup.py install`, from the visdom source directory.

That said, I just tried `pip_pypy install pybind11` with PyPy v5.7 and it fails with:
```
IOError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/usr/local/Cellar/pypy/HEAD-f3abf62ba364/libexec/include/eval.h'
```

Looks like the headers are being installed directly into the `include` directory instead if `include/pybind11` (thus trying to overwrite an existing file which is also called `eval.h`.). But I'm not sure if it's a bug on the pypy or pybind11 side.

The main reason I wanted us to pause and think about things here is to plan upfront how we expect this update mechanism to work for different installation methods.

My thought was we'd do what I described above during `prepare()` where we currently copy the file. My plan was to never prompt the user, but either silently update the file if there was no changes or print a warning about the `.conf.new` file. If there's a warning, we could easily print the warning on every run or just once.

My preference for a universal solution was because I think it'll take less time to implement and we'd have instructions that apply to all systems rather than having instructions be unique to the distro. With that said, if we have a somewhat simple and robust solution for the vast majority of our users, I don't care too much what we do here.

We should kick this conversation down the road, but for the purposes of presenting the other side while this issue sits here publicly, I'll weigh in. I support this issue given the current state of the rest of Certbot. Certbot provides help output like this:
```
  --redirect            Automatically redirect all HTTP traffic to HTTPS for
                        the newly authenticated vhost. (default: None)
```
It also asks questions like this:
```
Please choose whether HTTPS access is required or optional.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: Easy - Allow both HTTP and HTTPS access to these sites
2: Secure - Make all requests redirect to secure HTTPS access
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Select the appropriate number [1-2] then [enter] (press 'c' to cancel):
```
The `Easy` and `Secure` distinction sucks. Additionally, in this instance it's incorrect that `Easy` allows "both HTTP and HTTPS access to these sites." If you're site wasn't already set up for HTTP, it still won't be.

While the framing sucks when asked interactively (although the command line flag is very clear), the intent is to ask the user if they want an HTTP->HTTPS redirect. If they say yes, I think we should give it to them.

We certainly should not start listening on port 80 unless the user asked for it. Sounds like a good way to make people mad.

**Settings**
```
GUARDIAN_GET_CONTENT_TYPE = 'project.apps.core.utils.get_polymorphic_base_content_type'
```
**Code**
```
def get_polymorphic_base_content_type(obj):
    """
    Special helper function to return BASE polymorphic ctype id
    """
    if hasattr(obj, 'polymorphic_model_marker'):
        try:
            superclasses = list(obj.__class__.mro())
        except TypeError:
            # obj is an object so mro() need to be called with the obj.
            superclasses = list(obj.__class__.mro(obj))

        polymorphic_superclasses = list()
        for sclass in superclasses:
            if hasattr(sclass, 'polymorphic_model_marker'):
                polymorphic_superclasses.append(sclass)

        # PolymorphicMPTT adds an additional class between polymorphic and base class
        if hasattr(obj, 'can_have_children'):
            root_polymorphic_class = polymorphic_superclasses[-3]
        else:
            root_polymorphic_class = polymorphic_superclasses[-2]
        ctype = ContentType.objects.get_for_model(root_polymorphic_class)

    else:
        ctype = ContentType.objects.get_for_model(obj)

    return ctype
```

@mehrdadn and I just looked into this a bit more.

If you do the following

```python
import ray
ray.init()

class Foo(object):
    pass

@ray.remote
class Bar(Foo):
    def __init__(self):
        super(Bar, self).__init__()

b = Bar.remote()
```

the constructor task prints the following error.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/rkn/Workspace/ray/python/ray/worker.py", line 1841, in process_task
    worker.actors[task.actor_id().id()], *arguments)
  File "<ipython-input-4-bb5ad440612a>", line 4, in __init__
TypeError: super(type, obj): obj must be an instance or subtype of type
```

`type` is `Bar` and `obj.__class__` is `NewClass`. `NewClass` should only exist on the driver, but its showing up here because in the above code, the `Bar` in the line `super(Bar, self).__init__()` refers to the decorated `Bar`. That is `ray.remote(Bar)` returns a `NewClass`, which is what the `Bar` in the `super` call is referring to.

One possible solution is to be more eager and to pickle `Bar` inside of the remote decorator (which is what we do with remote functions).

Subject: [streamlink/streamlink] stream.hls: format string name input for parse_variant_playlist (#675)


This allows developers to use a format string to name the streams, which allows more descriptive names. eg. {pixels}_{bitrate} can be used to produce 720p_3500k, 720p_4500k, which is useful if the streams are available in multiple resolutions at the same bitrate.

This feature can also be used when using the builtin stream plugins, eg.

streamlink "hlsvariant://test.se/master.m3u8 name_fmt=\"{pixels}_{bitrate}\""

The limitation being that the name string has to be enclosed by " quotes.

This should go someway to fixing issues like #540<https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/540>.

> There's also a much more important race condition: because we spawn the server in the background, and then immediately attempt to connect to it, we might end up connecting before the server is actually started up and has bound its port. However I still make use of the `unused_tcp_port` for two things:

1) Getting a unused port and keeping it this way !
This is useful to test how a client would react if the server it should connect to is not available

2) Configuring a class that does connection to the server internally
In my usecase I have a client A and two servers (so each one started by a `trio.serve_tcp`) B&C.
A connects to B which in turn connects to C.
When I want to test the interface between A and B, I must provide the port where C is running as configuration when starting B. Using `unused_tcp_port` means I don't have to hack/patch B connection to C code to make it uses `trio.testing.open_stream_to_socket_listener`.

Looking at it more closely, perhaps the primary gap in `onnx.proto` for enabling something like this is the distinction made between the `operator` and `graph` namespaces. Eg: operators are (or can be) nothing more than references to other subgraphs, and operators with `op_type` names that are not resolved against the set of `graph`s in a ONNX file (module?) can resolve against the set of intrinsic names for primitive ops.

XLA has something similar to this now with their [`Call`](https://www.tensorflow.org/performance/xla/operation_semantics#call) op, but I believe this is closer to supporting anonymous functions, whereas for matching purposes it is important that ONNX represents named 'functions'.

I don't see why it is all that much work, you just pick a new name out of your operator namespace and just use it.

Perhaps the missing piece is that the validator needs to ignore operators that it doesn't understand. It seems useful, in this case, to have a "strict" and "relaxed" mode for the validator.

I agree. It sounds like a good idea.

(However, I think it does require some work in the backend implementation: the intention is to allow some operator implementations avoid computing some output if it is not required (e.g., for example RNN where we want only the final state and not the entire sequence of states); at the same time, we do not want to burden all operator implementations with having to check if some output is required or not (admittedly, this is relevant only when they compute multiple outputs). So, the annotation can play a useful role, but it is more of a backend implementation issue, and should probably not be in the ONNX spec.

I'd like to support passing both an OID or a tree so the user can pass in whatever variable they have in their code. Passing in a OID forces us to look it up in the repository, so we can be sure that the objects exist. Accepting a tree becomes harder. What happens when the tree comes from another repository instance, for example? We could just grab its OID and look it up in our repo, but that's not necessarily the best thing, as this foreign tree could still only reference objects that we do have.

This is probably a fringe case and saying "if you pass a `Tree`, then `tree->repo == self->repo` must be true" is a good compromise.

Now, what I've no idea how to do is accept several object types.
